title: Tool
name: NetScan
desc: a small c program that scans your network for devices and open ports. it also displays hostname(if found) vendor and more.

aboutParagraphs: NetScan is a command-line network scanner built from scratch in C. It auto-detects your local\n subnet, ping-sweeps it to find live hosts, and identifies each device through DNS, mDNS, NetBIOS, and SSDP lookups, falling back to a MAC vendor lookup when nothing else resolves. Open ports are found with a multi-threaded TCP connect-scan, with lightweight banner grabbing for identifying running services. Results can be exported to JSON or CSV, and a diff mode flags new or missing devices between scans. I built it to get hands-on with raw sockets, threading, and Linux networking internals in C — there's still plenty I'd like to add, but it's a solid, working tool as it stands.

title: Pi Project
name: Raspberry Pi Wi-Fi to Ethernet Router
desc: Configured a Raspberry Pi 3B as a Wi-Fi-to-Ethernet router using NetworkManager, dnsmasq, and nftables.

aboutParagraphs: This project turns a Raspberry Pi 3B into a standalone router that takes in internet over Wi-Fi and shares it out over Ethernet. The Pi's Ethernet interface is configured as a static gateway (10.10.10.1/24), with dnsmasq handing out DHCP leases to anything plugged in — directly or through a switch — and nftables masquerading that traffic out through the Wi-Fi interface, so wired clients get full internet access with no dependency on the host network's own router. Most of the work was less about the networking theory and more about fighting the current state of Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm): it's moved fully to NetworkManager, so the classic dhcpcd-based setup guides don't apply anymore, IP forwarding now lives in /etc/sysctl.d/ instead of the old sysctl.conf, and dnsmasq needed bind-dynamic instead of bind-interfaces to stop it from failing before the Ethernet interface was up. I also had to make sure an existing Tailscale install on the Pi wasn't stepping on the new NAT rules, since it manages its own iptables-nft table. Verified it all by pulling a laptop's Wi-Fi entirely and confirming it still got an IP from the Pi and had full internet through Ethernet alone.
